data.title: NQP: Useful For PA Residents But Care Is Needed
data.description:
Mechanics matter

The nuts and bolts of how things are constructed, how they work, are important. This becomes more so the more complex the machinery is. Thus it's a darn good idea to understand how something is working internally before we decide whether this is something we want to use, something likely to achieve a goal in line with our own desires.

This is as true of financial instruments as it is of any other piece of complex machinery. Therefore we need an explanation of those moving parts before we can decide whether we're interested. Or not, as the case may be.

Nuveen Pennsylvania Quality Municipal Income Fund (NYSE:NQP)

The most obvious thing we should say at the start here is that NQP is aimed at taxpaying residents of Pennsylvania. If you're not one of those then this instrument just isn't going to be of great interest. So, unless you're really enamoured of my prose, if you're not paying tax in PA now would be a good time to do something else other than read this.

This is a municipal bond fund. Which in itself requires a little explanation.

There are, essentially, three types of bond. Treasuries are lending to the Federal Government and as Uncle Sam hasn't gone bust this past two centuries (although, as other countries have proven, this is no certainty for the future) we accept to assume that there is no credit risk here. It's not wholly and exactly true but it's a basic underpinning of how our financial markets work that we act like it is.

There is still though interest rate risk. A bond issued yesterday, when interest rates were 3%, is worth a different amount from one issued today, when interest rates are 4%. That difference in capital value is whatever the net present value of $3 or $4 a year on each $100 of holding is. Clearly, that depends upon how long the bond lasts for (the "tenor") for a 30 year bond will change in value more than a 30 day one.

It's also true that coupons - the interest rate paid - on bonds rise the longer that tenor of the bond. So we've a little difficulty here for of course we'd like to maximise our income. But that means having to take on more interest rate risk. There is no cute way out of this either. We simply do have to carry that extra interest rate risk if we want to gain the higher coupon.

The second grouping is corporates, lending to companies. They can - as we all know - go bust so this introduces credit risk. Therefore corporate bonds should pay more than Treasuries and they do.

There is a way around higher credit risk. It's not a complete solution but it aids, and that's diversification. Hold little bits of many different issues/issuers and if one goes bust that's an annoyance not a disaster. For most bonds - all three types - we can usually buy $1,000 lots at issue and trade them in at redemption. But the secondary markets are a little different. They can be thin, illiquid, and also in rather larger sizes. The European corporate bond markets trade in $200k par value minimum lot sizes for example.

The way around this is to buy into a bond fund. The fund is large enough to be diversified even in those secondary markets, we own a fraction of the fund.

The third type of bond is municipals. Lendng to subnational governmental bodies - states to the local water board. These can go bust - the default rate is low, perhaps 0.1% by one estimate but it exists - so munis should pay more than Treasuries even if maybe less than corporates. That's not how it works out - munis pay less than Uncle Sam.

The trick is that municipals' income is Federal income tax free and, usually, a within state bond is state income tax free for within state income tax payers.

That's how we get bond funds holding municipals which are aimed at the residents of a particular state, as here with NQP, aimed at residents of Pennsylvania.

But how good is a muni fund?

Because the value (OK, the added value) of a muni fund is in the tax break the, erm, valuation of that extra added value depends upon the tax rate that would be charged upon a taxable income. This will vary for different taxpayers as tax rates do vary dependent upon income bands and so on. So, what we do is convert to a "tax equivalent yield". What would a taxable investment have to pay as a yield to equal the post tax cash in hand we're getting as a result of the tax break? Fortunately people work out tables of this for us:

There's no exact number at which munis become "the right" decision. Some people really hate paying taxes and of course there are those who so love government that they'll happily pay more. Well, there are those who tell us so that is. But the usual rule of thumb is that if your marginal income tax rate - the rate on your next $ of income - is 24% or above then you probably will benefit from munis as opposed to taxable yields.

So, if you're currently making $50k or more and are resident in PA then NQP could well be of interest to you. Well, assuming that you're saving, you want income from your savings and so on.

Fund details

NQP is a closed end fund. This means that our money, when we buy, does not go into the fund. Rather, we buy off a selling stockholder. One implication of this is that the fund cannot suffer a run, a redemption cascade - there's a certain safety to that. Although, given how liquid municipal bonds are that's not hugely a concern anyway. Another point is that a closed fund can be leveraged and NQP is. 42.95%. If the coupon on the bonds held is higher than the short term borrowing rate then this will juice returns.

The third implication is that a closed end fund can move well off net asset value.

A usual solution to this is that the managers will boost the yield by increasing the distribution to include some "return of capital". This should bring the net asset value down closer to market price. Of course, it's also possible that this doesn't, quite, happen and all that does is that the discount to NAV stays constant and the market price drops. Or even a combination of both effects.

We rather have a choice as to what blend of those two stories we wish to apply here.

More details

The top holdings are:

We can see the full list here. There are 294 holdings so they are actively managing exposure, this is not the entire universe - as an index fund would typically hold - of available bonds. Fees are 0.66% a year which is also an indication of active management - an index fund would be a little over one tenth of that.

Credit ratings of holdings:

And maturities:

We can see what the investment policy is from those two charts. Go long tenor in order to maximise coupon and income but at the same time be selective on credit quality so as to reduce credit risk. So, little credit risk, rather more interest rate risk. This is neither good nor bad it's simply a choice.

So, how well does NQP do?

The headline yield there is 8.67%. Which is really pretty darn good - so good it's off the chart of those calculations of tax-e yield. But we should also take that with more than a little salt. For as we've also seen when the NAV discount blows out a fund can decide to boost the reported yield by return of capital. This, in theory, lowers that discount. It's also possible that there are less informed investors who simply buy on the yield and don't realise how much of their supposed income is really just that RoC. If that were true then the effect would come - that reduction in NAV discount - from a rising stock price. We might even think that some of both happens.

But we, of course, are not less informed investors. So we can look up how much of that distribution is return of capital:

For the first 6 months of the year 45% of the (OK, 43.89) distribution is return of capital. That gives us a 4.77% "real distribution". That's what our money would be earning in the fund. To be able to compare this with other options that's the right - or very close to being the right - number to be using. And that's actually pretty good. For non-taxpayers it's still - a little - above what we can gain pretty much risk free but taxable in USFR. Or, again a little, over what we can get with less interest rate risk in SHYM but again non-taxable.

For higher rate taxpayers it becomes very attractive indeed.

Why I'm wrong

My biggest worry here would be that single state muni funds are diversified, yes, across different issuers. But they're all, rather and sort of, linked issuers. If the state finances themselves start to fail then we'd not assume that all of the instate borrowers would be unaffected. So I think we're not wholly diversified even though we're in a couple of hundred issues. But that's something to worry about in extremis, not now.

My opinion

I generally dislike the use of return of capital to boost distributions. I'm not - as above - wholly sure that the usual story wholly works, that this reduces net asset value down to market. I think there can be some buying which boosts market price - which, obviously, disappears when the discount being addressed does.

But my bigger worry is that it can mislead. If people don't realise that near half of what they think is their income is, in fact, their own capital coming back then they can misallocate their spending decisions. They need to be putting a substantial portion - like, about that near half - of their supposed income back into investments in order to maintain their capital for the long term.

But as long as that's understood I think it's fine. For, you know, us informed sophisticates.

The thing is that even after adjusting for RoC the return is still substantial. So, NQP is a good buy for a certain very specific set of investors. Higher income tax rate paying investors in Pennsylvania, those looking for income from their investments, could and should do well here. Out of state, obviously, it's of no great use. So, if you meet the investor profile then this could be a good buy.

data.title: Fed Narrows 11th Consecutive Quarterly Loss Due To Quantitative Tightening
data.description:
Last Friday, when all eyes from the financial markets were focused on Fed Chair Powell’s remarks at the Kansas City Fed’s Jackson Hole Economic Symposium, the Fed quietly released their operating results for the quarter ending June 30, 2025.

While most Fed Watchers tried to parse Chair Powell’s comments about employment, inflation, and how the Fed might respond to the conflict in direction between the two key economic indicators of their dual mandate, not one word was uttered about the Fed’s precarious financial position.

What the quarterly results showed, however, was that the Fed lost money on an operating basis for the 11th consecutive quarter. These are realized losses.

Further, no attention was given to the almost $1 trillion unrealized loss the Fed is carrying on their balance sheet.

Why The Fed Is Losing Money

The main reason the Fed has been losing money is that they have an asset/liability mismatch.

Simply put, this means that the Fed owns long-term fixed rate securities (Treasury Bonds and Mortgage-Backed Securities) and funds them with short-term variable rate liabilities (Bank Reserves and Reverse Repurchases Agreements.)

Creating an asset/liability mismatch is a fundamental error in bank management, as it exposes the institution to a rise in interest rates. When interest rates go up, the cost of the bank’s liabilities increases, while the market value of their assets decline.

The Fed started losing money in 4Q22 when the interest expense they were paying on their short-term liabilities rose above the income they were earning on their fixed rate assets, due to the Fed tightening to fight inflation. They have been losing money ever since.

Quarterly Operating Results

The Fed lost $6.2 billion from operations in the second quarter, a significant improvement from last year’s second quarter operating loss of $16.9 billion.

This was their smallest quarterly deficit since they last made a profit in 3Q22.

While Interest Income fell by -11.4% to $39.6 billion y/y, Interest Expense plunged by -23.7% to $45.1 billion y/y.

There are three factors that have caused the Fed’s quarterly operating results to gradually improve:

  1. The Fed has reduced their asset/liability mismatch through Quantitative Tightening (QT).
  2. The cost of the Fed’s liabilities is 100 basis points lower than a year ago.
  3. The average yield on the Fed’s Treasury holdings has increased by 17 basis points y/y.

The Fed Has Reduced Their Asset / Liability Mismatch Through QT

The mismatch came into being when the Fed began their Quantitative Easing (QE) Program during the Great Financial Crisis of 2008 to inject liquidity into the system. The Fed deviated from their historical practice of buying short maturity Treasury Securities as they purchased longer maturity Treasury Notes and Bonds, and Mortgage-Backed Securities. The Fed’s balance Sheet expanded tenfold during QE, rising from less than $900 billion in total assets to almost $9 trillion at the peak.

The liquidity injection was always intended to be temporary, and when the Fed began to fight the rise in inflation, they implemented their Policy Normalization Plan. Part of the plan was to reduce the size of their balance sheet, more commonly referred to as QT, thereby draining the liquidity (Bank Reserves and Reverse Repurchase Agreements) that was causing the inflation.

As the Fed’s assets increased through QE, so too did their liquidity. Pre-QE, the liquidity on the Fed balance sheet was $54 billion, and it grew more than 100-fold to a high of $6.1 trillion.

Since QT began, the Fed has reduced both their total assets and their liquidity by $2.3 trillion from their peak. This has caused the asset/liability mismatch to narrow, for in percentage terms, the liquidity has declined by 38% while total assets have shrunk by only 26%.

Total assets are currently $6.6 trillion, while liquidity stands at $3.7 trillion. Total assets are at their lowest level since 2Q20, while liquidity is at its lowest level since 1Q21.

The Cost Of The Fed’s Liabilities Is 100 Basis Points Lower Than A Year Ago

The Fed cut the Fed Funds rate by 100 basis points during the final third of 2024 to the range of 4.25-4.50%. The cost of the Fed’s variable rate liabilities is closely tied to Fed Funds, so last year, the Fed paid an average of 5.33% on their liabilities, while this year they paid only 4.33%. This resulted in substantial savings in interest expense to the Fed and helped narrow the Fed’s Negative Net Interest Margin.

The market’s preoccupation with Chair Powell’s comments at Jackson Hole was to try to glean when the Fed will next cut rates. Based on his statement “with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance,” the market is looking for another rate cut in September.

CME FedWatch is projecting a 90% probability of a 25 basis point cut.

The Average Yield On The Fed’s Treasury Securities Has Been Rising

When the Fed began QT, they were allowing $60 billion in maturing Treasury Securities to runoff each month. As the Fed’s balance sheet has shrunk, they have twice tapered their monthly runoff. First, on June 1, 2024, the monthly Treasury runoff was cut to $25 billion per month, then, effective April 1, 2025, the monthly Treasury runoff was tapered again to only $5 billion per month. The Fed tapered their Treasury runoffs to slow down the process as they approached their desired level of Ample Reserves, to prevent the type of liquidity crisis that popped up in September 2019, when QT was shrinking too abruptly.

As I mentioned in my previous Seeking Alpha article, “Fed to Begin Tapering Their Balance Sheet Runoff And Other Observations”:

The Fed has been clear in why they are tapering their QT policy normalization program. The last time QT was attempted, in 2017-2019, the Fed went too fast and they experienced a liquidity problem. Fed Funds spiked from 2% to almost 10%. This caused them to immediately stop QT and reverse policy to stem the liquidity imbalance.

The Fed learned their lesson from that experience, so as a result, they want to move more slowly to prevent a recurrence. Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan, at a recent Monetary Policy Conference, stated "slowing…doesn’t mean stopping. In fact…proceeding more gradually may allow the Fed to eventually get to a smaller balance sheet."

In doing so, the Fed began reinvesting the proceeds of Treasury Securities that were maturing above the new monthly cap. These mostly were securities that were purchased when yields were around 2.00% and were now being reinvested in Treasury Securities yielding the current market rate of around 4.00%. The net impact of the reinvestment of maturing Treasuries at a higher yield was to raise the overall yield of the Fed’s Treasury position. At June 30, 2024, the Fed’s Treasury holdings were yielding 2.22%, and at June 30, 2025, the Fed’s smaller Treasury position, due to QT, was now yielding 2.39%.

How The Fed’s Average Weekly Operating Loss Has Improved

Progress in the Fed’s weekly operating loss can be seen in the following chart. As mentioned above, the Fed’s shrinking asset/liability mismatch and narrower Negative Net Interest Margin have resulted in an improvement in the Fed’s operating results.

The following chart shows the 4-week average weekly loss to smooth out technical distortions. During last year’s second quarter, the Fed was losing roughly -$1.3 billion per week, while for 2Q25 the Fed’s average weekly was approximately -$600 million per week.

Cumulative Operating Losses Are Carried As A Deferred Asset On the Fed’s Balance Sheet

The Fed’s cumulative loss from operations over the 11 quarters is -$234 billion. This is more than five times the Fed’s total capital of $45.5 billion.

Fortunately, the Fed uses a lenient accounting system that allows them to carry the loss on their balance sheet as a deferred asset, so their capital account is not impaired.

For a detailed analysis please see my Seeking Alpha article, “How The Fed Turns A $105 Billion Loss Into An Asset.”

Unrealized SOMA Portfolio Losses

The Fed is also carrying another loss on its Balance Sheet.

The market value of the Fed’s bond holdings in its SOMA portfolio is significantly below the cost of purchasing those securities. This is because the bulk of the securities were purchased during the Fed’s QE period when interest rates were much lower than they are now.

As can be seen in the chart below, since QT began, the yield on the 10-year Treasury Note has more than doubled to 4.26%.

As a result, the market value of the Fed’s Treasury Securities and their Mortgage-Backed Securities is $927 billion below their cost. The Fed uses another lenient accounting treatment that allows them to carry their bond holdings at Amortized Cost. Consequently, this loss in value of their assets only shows up in a footnote on their balance sheet.

The market value of the SOMA portfolio represents a -14% loss from the cost of purchasing the securities.

Fed Policy Going Forward

Fed Governor Christopher Waller, who is a candidate to replace Jay Powell when his term as Fed Chair expires in May 2026, gave a very candid assessment of the Fed’s balance sheet last month in a speech before the Dallas Fed.

For the first time, a senior Fed official publicly acknowledged the balance sheet maturity mismatch and the losses it has caused. He also was specific about when the Fed might end QT.

QT has been a Fed policy for three and one-half years, and many are trying to predict when it will end. Chair Powell has been quite vague about this, stating in his recent Semiannual Congressional Testimony “We’re trying to stop a little bit above what we consider ample, and we think we’re meaningfully above that now…we can’t put a number on it because you can’t directly know the demand for reserves other than by observing behavior in the market and then putting a little bit of a buffer on it.”

Waller was more definitive. He stated he feels the Fed will continue with QT until Bank Reserves fall to $2.7 trillion. Currently they are $3.3 trillion. He arrives at this figure by comparing today’s situation to the last time the Fed implemented QT in 2019. The trouble then began when reserves fell below 8% of GDP, so he picked 9% of GDP as a buffer. GDP is roughly $30 trillion.

Governor Waller went on further, saying that he believes that the Fed will settle with a balance sheet of assets totaling $5.8 trillion.

He gets to this figure by adding the three main liabilities of the Fed: Currency, which historically has been the Fed’s largest liability, is $2.3 trillion. The Treasury General Account, which is the Treasury’s checking account at the Fed, typically averages $780 billion. And Bank Reserves, as mentioned above, will settle at $2.7 trillion. This totals $5.8 trillion.

With current assets at $6.6 trillion and a monthly runoff rate of $20 billion ($5 billion in Treasuries and $15 billion in MBS), Waller’s projection implies QT will continue for another 40 months. This is a much longer period of time than many were projecting.

Conclusion

When the Fed implemented QE to battle the Great Financial Crisis of 2008, they deviated from their traditional policy and created an asset/liability mismatch as they expanded their balance sheet to add liquidity to the system.

Since 2022, they have been trying to Normalize Policy by executing QT as they battle the inflation that appeared due to that liquidity injection.

The cost to the Fed has been huge as they’ve tightened monetary policy.

The Fed has incurred a cumulative operating loss of -$234 billion, which they carry as a deferred asset on their balance sheet, and they also carry an unrealized loss of -$927 billion in their SOMA portfolio.

The Fed has made headway, as their quarterly losses have declined due to the asset/liability mismatch shrinking through QT, hence their desire for it to continue.

The Fed insists that their operating losses and unrealized SOMA losses will not affect their ability to conduct monetary policy.

While this may be true in the short run, the Fed’s financial impairment has contributed to a huge political battle with the current administration that is threatening the Fed’s independence.

The Fed has never been under so much political pressure.

Time will tell if they are able to maintain their independence.

data.title: Updating Gold Ratios To Cyclical Markets
data.description:
Important gold ratios to markets that are more cyclical

When gold rises again vs. these markets the counter-cyclical economic view will generally be back on. Gold has far more utility (insurance, risk-off liquidity, relative value retention) during economically troubled times than cyclical assets like stocks and commodities.

Gold/SPX (U.S. stock market)

A sweet consolidation as the market angst of the spring has gone bye-bye. This is a lovely consolidation that was much needed as gold got over-pumped to the upside (and stocks to the downside) in April.

Gold/Global (World, ex-U.S.)

Another constructive downward consolidation in the monetary metal vs. global stock markets. Sure, it’s below the moving averages, which are about to Death Cross. You’d do well to tune such noise out. It’ll probably spring a rally in the ratio and in my opinion, end the correction.

Gold/CRB Commodity Index

A nice, flat consolidation riding the uptrending 50 day moving average. Counter-cyclical gold laying in wait to out-perform cyclical commodities into the next counter-cycle.

Gold/WTI Crude Oil

Another uptrending counter-cyclical marker, which is another way of saying another indicator trending toward a rude interruption, at least, of this phase of the economic cycle. The gold mining industry would benefit, fundamentally (product up in relation to cost input).

Gold/Copper

The market got a lot of noise injected by Trump expectations vs. what the Trump jawbone actually said (about copper related tariffs). But the moving averages show uptrends in Au/Cu the whole while. Star counter-cyclical metal bullish vs. star cyclical metal.

Gold/Silver Ratio

Moving on to a couple more complicated items, the post-April pullback in the GSR has given life to many “SGR trades” as I call them. Those with an implied tailwind with a rising Silver/Gold ratio. The GSR is still trapped below the moving averages and so, those trades drag on. But at such time as GSR may start to bull and drag the US dollar along with it, best to consider backing away from the punch bowl and stay away from the pool, because it’ll have a strange brown object floating in there.

Gold/Inflation Expectations

This ratio is sliding along bullishly. The indication is that inflation has not been a major problem since 2023. The indication is that the opposite of inflation could even come to be feared before the next inflationary phase.

data.title: RTX Corporation: You Should Not Ignore This Aerospace And Defense Powerhouse
data.description:
RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX), a major player in the aerospace and defense sector, has outperformed the market with a 10.6% stock gain compared to the S&P 500’s 3.8% increase since my previous report. The company released its Q2 2025 earnings in July. I now review the results and provide an updated price target for investors.

RTX Posts Robust Sales Growth

Second quarter sales rose 9% to $21.58 billion, exceeding analysts's expectations by $950 million. The growth was driven by commercial aftermarket sales and good growth in commercial OEM sales despite lower volumes on the Boeing 737 MAX program. Defense sales also showed good growth with a 6% jump. Free cash flow generation was mute, driven by a work stoppage at Pratt & Whitney, but this will be recovered in the balance of the year. Other pressures on cash flow came from a $250 million compensation rendered related to the GTF issues and $125 million in tariff headwinds. Adjusted EPS grew 11% to $1.56, exceeding estimates by $0.13.

Collins Aerospace Grows Aftermarket And Defense Sales

Colins Aerospace sales grew 9% to $7.6 billion with strong growth in commercial aftermarket sales and defense. The aftermarket sales continue to be driven by high asset utilization and limited supply of new airplanes. The limited supply of new airplanes also resulted in Commercial OEM sales growing only 1%, with lower 737 MAX shipset volumes being more than offset by improvements in Boeing 787 volumes. Defense sales were up 11%, driven by the F-35 and the Survivable Airborne Operations Center program. Profits increased 9% to $1.25 billion in adjusted basis, showing stable margins balancing lower R&D, better commercial aftermarket and defense volumes, a strong defense mix with tariff impacts, and an unfavorable commercial OEM mix.

Pratt & Whitney Grows Double Digits

Pratt & Whitney reports sales growth of 12% to $7.63 billion, driven by 19% growth in commercial aftermarket volumes. While Collins saw flat OEM sales, Pratt saw its commercial OEM sales grow by 15%, driven by volume and mix. The defense revenues also contrasted with Collin’s performance, posting flat revenues driven by flat F-35 engine volumes and contract award timing. Adjusted operating profits grew 13%, offsetting the impact of tariffs and the strike at Pratt as well as an unfavorable aftermarket mix.

Raytheon Shows Positive Results From Productivity Transformation

Raytheon reported sales growth of 6% to $7 billion, driven by higher volumes, and we saw a strong improvement in margins from 10.8% to 11.6%, driving operating profits up to $809 million. At times, the Raytheon business lagged from a performance perspective, but the company has implemented productivity measures through a digital backbone that is paying off.

RTX Q2 Earnings Show Tariffs Offsetting Production Efficiency and Lower R&D

What is unfortunate for RTX is that its progress in driving margins up in Raytheon through implementing a digital core and benefiting from lower R&D and good volumes in its other segments is being offset by tariffs. The company has absorbed $125 million in tariff hits out of a revised figure of $500 million. I believe that RTX’s earnings show which parties are suffering from the tariffs, and those are mostly customers, U.S. corporations, and shareholders.

RTX Sees Higher Sales Growth, EPS Revised Down On Tariff Impact

Despite tariff challenges, there has been a positive revision to the full-year outlook. The company has revised its sales growth from 4%-6% to 6%-7%. This is driven by Collins showing high single-digit sales instead of mid-single digits, Pratt moved up from high single-digit growth to low double-digit, and the outlook for Raytheon remained stable at mid-single digit growth. Adjusted EPS has been revised down by $0.20 to $5.80-$5.95, driven by a $0.30 tariff headwind partially offset by tax benefits. Free cash flow guidance remained stable at $7-$7.5 billion. Tariffs are now expected to have a $500 million negative impact net of offsets, an improvement compared to the $850 million expected earlier.

RTX is leveraging the USMCA agreement and making existing parts USMCA compliant while also leveraging military duty-free exemptions.

RTX Stock Is Undervalued Against Aerospace Peers

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

In terms of EBITDA, estimates for 2025 have barely changed. In 2026, there is <1% revision upward and 1.7% upward for 2027. This drives the CAGR from 3.4% to 3.9% with a total EBITDA generation increase of 0.8% between 2025 and 2027. On free cash flow, estimates have improved, driven by lower expected impact of tariffs this year. This drives a 3.3% higher free cash flow than initially expected this year. For 2026, there is a small increase of less than 1% and a 3.3% increase in 2027. This improved the CAGR to 27.3%, and total free cash flow generation has been revised up by 2.4%.

Based on FY26 earnings and a valuation between the company median and the peer group, I am raising my price target for RTX to $190.60, implying 19% upside.

Conclusion: RTX Corporation Stock Remains Attractive

Second quarter earnings showed pressure from tariffs. Absent of tariffs, we would have likely seen margin expansion as the company is growing its volumes. Even with tariff impacts, I expect that the estimated increases in airplane production, most notably the Boeing 737 MAX, will positively drive earnings going forward, while we also note that demand for aftermarket services, missiles, and missile defense systems remains robust. As a result, I believe the stock remains compelling for investors looking to benefit from sales momentum in the commercial airplane and defense industries.

data.title: Atour Lifestyle Q2: The Growth Outlook Accelerates
data.description:
Atour Lifestyle Holdings Limited (NASDAQ:ATAT) reported the company’s Q2 results on the 26th of August. The Chinese hotel chain franchiser continues to expand at an accelerating as the company's another 2025 growth guidance raise suggests. At the same time, marketing investments continue to drive brand recognition for Atour’s retail business.

I remained at a Buy rating in my previous April article on the stock, titled “Atour Lifestyle: The Growth Story Remains Undervalued”. The stock has since returned 58%, well above the S&P 500’s return of 21%.

Atour Q2: The Growth Outlook Accelerates

Atour continued rapid growth in Q2. Revenues grew by 37.4% year-on-year to RMB 2.47 billion ($345 million), beating Wall Street’s consensus by $19 million through accelerated momentum. The quarter’s adjusted EPS of $0.42 beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.04.

The main hotel revenue stream has continued to grow; Atour’s franchise expansion remains on track. Manachised hotel revenues reached RMB 1.30 billion, up by 26.5% year-on-year as Atour’s hotel footprint expanded to a total of 1824 hotels. The number of hotels is up by 29.2% year-on-year. Most importantly, the growth pipeline remains incredibly good with 816 additional manachised hotels under development at the end of Q2.

Underlying operational metrics were fair in the hotel segment. The average daily room rate “ARD” saw pressure that again slightly weakened growth, this time being down by -1.8% to RMB 433. The pace of ADR pressure has started to moderate from a -5.8% decline in 2024 and a -2.8% decline in Q1, though – travel agency operator Trip.com (TCOM) also noted stabilizing industry-wide hotel room rates in China in the May earnings call, and it seems that such a notation is holding true. I believe that gradually stabilizing ADR is very positive for Atour; while not very critical to Atour’s immediate topline growth level, stabilizing rates should encourage franchisees to invest more in new hotels as the earnings outlook improves. With such a dynamic, a stabilizing ADR could contribute well towards Atour's long-term growth.

More negatively, hotel occupancy did decline by 200 basis points year-on-year to 76.4%. The decline reflects increasing competition and a volatile macroeconomic backdrop, not being detrimental to Atour’s longer-term outlook yet. Increasing competition seems to continue weighing on ADR and occupancy, but Atour has overall continued to maintain a healthy mix in spite of an increasingly challenging competitive landscape.

Retail revenues continue to drive even faster topline growth. Atour’s brands reached RMB 965 million in retail revenues for Q2 alone, up by 79.8% year-on-year. Atour’s brand recognition continues to grow and the company continues to innovate its product lineup to drive further growth. Notably, retail revenues did decline sequentially, but Atour has also previously experienced quarterly fluctuations in the retail segment revenue performance. A growing hotel footprint works as a growing platform for brand recognition over the long term, likely to drive further momentum to the retail business in upcoming quarters and years.

Profitability came in at a fairly good level. Adjusted EBITDA grew by 37.7% year-on-year to RMB 610 million, reflecting rapid topline growth and increased gross margins. The hotel gross margin increased by 260 basis points year-on-year to 38.3%, and the retail segment gross margin increased by 270 basis points to 53.3% through increased scale. Operating expense growth did offset a good part of margin gains, though - Atour increased marketing expenses massively to RMB 393 million, up by 74.9% year-on-year to drive retail segment growth. As long as the retail segment continues to grow, I don’t believe such marketing investments to be cause for concern.

Atour’s outlook remains very good. The company now guides 30% revenue growth for 2025, up from the previous 25-30% guidance range. As I wrote in my recent article on Trip.com, China is opening up the country for tourism with visa-free policies and social media visibility. Inbound tourism has continued to surge, in turn boosting the hospitality industry’s outlook while Atour continues to expand its nationwide presence. Investments in retail segment marketing should also contribute well to future growth.

Atour’s Valuation Has Upside Left

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Atour. I have raised my revenue expectations to a 2024-2034 CAGR of 13.4% with 3% perpetual growth after the estimate period. I still assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at 25%, and that Atour converts after-tax earnings well into free cash flow through the capital light manachised hotel operating model.

The estimates put Atour’s fair value at $51.1, 39% above the stock price at the time of writing – as Atour’s growth has even accelerated, the stock still undervalues Atour’s long-term earnings potential. I believe that tailwinds continue to make the stock an attractive investment at the current price.

The fair value estimate is up from $43.1 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.13%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t expect Atour to draw a noteworthy amount of debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.27% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.25% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for China. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.04. With a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.13%.

Takeaway

Atour’s Q2 report was good as revenue growth accelerated. The hotel expansion pipeline remains healthy even though the ADR and occupancy have continued to see some pressure, and earnings continue to benefit from scale. Retail momentum remains very healthy as Atour has invested massively in increased marketing. The stock still undervalues long-term earnings potential, and I remain with a Buy rating for ATAT stock.

data.title: BlackRock International Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 6.49% (Institutional shares) and 6.39% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s underperformance of its benchmark was largely due to overweight positions and stock selection in the industrials and health care sectors, along with investment decisions in the financials sector. Volatile market conditions, narrow international market leadership, and limited exposures to key sectors that helped drive the rally weighed on performance.
  • The fund reduced its U.S. exposure and off-benchmark risk while narrowing its underweight allocations to Asia and emerging markets. Simultaneously, we refined sector positioning, trimming lower-conviction stocks in the industrials and materials sectors, diversifying financials sector exposure, and consolidating high-conviction positions in the information technology and consumer discretionary sectors to enhance resilience and alignment.

Portfolio management

Olivia Treharne, Stephen Andrews & Molly Greenen

Investment approach

Invests primarily in a diversified portfolio of equity securities of companies outside the United States with emphasis on stocks of companies fund management believes are undervalued or have good prospects for earnings growth.

Contributors Detractors

The position in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company was beneficial, led by robust demand for artificial intelligence and high-performance computing chips. The company’s revenue rose strongly in May while it maintained its margin guidance. Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) was helpful due to strong first-quarter results and margins, though we exited the position to reduce off- benchmark risk and rebalance exposure. Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP) performed well given strong first-quarter volumes, though the holding was sold in May following a downward revision to its earnings-per-share guidance. Concerns that tariffs and trade restrictions would weigh on future volumes further undermined our investment thesis.

AstraZeneca, a U.K. pharmaceutical company, detracted due to health care sector weakness that stemmed from tariff concerns and regulatory scrutiny. While we trimmed the position, we remained constructive given the company’s strong first-quarter results and robust pipeline. Waste Connections (WCN) was unhelpful due to weather-driven volume declines and sector rotation, despite its strong defensive and margin profile. Shimano lagged after weak first-quarter earnings due to high inventories and soft demand. We reallocated the capital to higher-conviction stocks with better near-term visibility.

Further insight

The first half of 2025 proved challenging for the fund amid heightened market volatility and unexpected policy shifts, particularly in the United States, where assumptions of continued economic exceptionalism came under pressure. Despite equities reaching record highs, fiscal concerns and a weaker U.S. dollar have prompted a reassessment of regional allocations. We have seen increased investor interest in international diversification. We remain focused on the long-term potential of the high-quality businesses that we believe offer resilience and are best positioned to navigate uncertainty and deliver sustainable growth, even as short-term market dynamics continue to evolve.

data.title: Eurozone Sentiment Dips But Remains In Line With Q3 Strength
data.description:
By Bert Colijn

This morning’s batch of eurozone data seems to be in line with some cautious acceleration of growth in the third quarter. But after the rosy picture painted by the PMI last week, the European Commission’s sentiment indicator provides a more nuanced view of the economy: things are somewhat better than in the second quarter, but don’t expect a vibrant growth rate either.

Economic sentiment slightly weaker than in July

After the PMI reflected a surprisingly upbeat reading of the eurozone economy last week, the European Commission’s economic sentiment indicator adds a bit of nuance to the picture. A tick down from 95.2 to 94.9 in the headline index shows that expectations of a jump in growth would be overdrawn. The decline was mainly due to lower confidence among consumers, but services and construction sentiment indicators were also slightly down.

The improvement in manufacturing sentiment was smaller than for the PMI, but even so, industry did become more upbeat on orders, for example. And production improved by a lot according to the survey, although production expectations for the months ahead ticked down again. Still, that is in line with stronger third-quarter manufacturing output.

For services, demand weakened again and is now below the already soft second-quarter average. That means that so far, limited growth can be expected from the service sector in the third quarter, according to the survey. This will surely keep growth muted given the size of the sector.

Bank lending growth still modestly accelerating

The European Central Bank's bank lending data showed a continued acceleration of year-on-year growth. Even though lending growth has been very volatile from month to month recently, the growth rate for July compared to July last year increased for both households and non-financial corporates to a rate of 2.4% and 2.8% respectively. Historically, this remains modest, but it is still likely to translate into somewhat higher business investment.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Smith Douglas Homes Is Well Run And Poised For Growth
data.description:
Smith Douglas Homes Corp. (NYSE:SDHC) is a relatively small homebuilder with an intriguing business model. The company has a number of operational and financial metrics that cause it to stick out amongst much larger peers. With a market cap just north of $1 billion, this underfollowed developer offers a great opportunity to purchase a well-run company, which should benefit greatly when the housing market recovers.

Business Model

Smith Douglas Homes was founded in 2008 and went public via a traditional NYSE IPO in January, 2024. They operate a land-light homebuilding model characterized by purchasing lot option contracts and outsourcing lot development to land banks in order to reduce capital requirements. Their houses are marketed towards the lower end of the market with a focus on first-time home buyers as well as empty nesters looking to downsize. Their average sales price in 2024 was $340k.

None of the characteristics in the paragraph above are particularly unique amongst large regional homebuilders, but the company does have some distinctions that caught my eye. Start with their 56 day cycle time (the time it takes them to build a house), which compares with 132 days at Lennar and 100 days at NVR. Other homebuilders are cagey about disclosing cycle times, but the average cycle time mentioned on industry earnings calls seems to be in the range of 4-6 months. SDHC’s strikingly low cycle time is partly due to the type of house they build – relatively small square footage with uncomplicated designs. They also focus on preselling the vast majority of their homes before building them, which reduces mid-project customization delays. Still, it's an impressive feat and an ode to management’s focus on efficiency.

This efficient process starts from the beginning of their homebuilding process, with buyers choosing from a limited number of floor plans and a few customization options. 87% of 2024 closings were derived from fewer than 30 floor plans. By narrowing the number of floorplans they build, SDHC is able to improve efficiency and repeatability as construction crews and project managers become familiar with building the same house several times.

The construction process is managed through the company’s SMART Builder enterprise resource planning system, which allows the company to manage all aspects of the home building process and provides management with visibility at a very granular level. This transparent process-oriented approach allows SDHC to operate in a decentralized manor by setting up what they call “RTeams”. These teams operate in a collaborative environment in which each RTeam works together and with trade partners to solve problems, share best practices, and work efficiently. Each RTeam is organized to start one house per working day, which equates to 210/year. This operating model also provides a structure for organic growth. As each division grows, they can add RTeams to ramp up production in a staged approach.

This strategy has allowed SDHC to post gross margins which rival or beat many of their larger competitors.

This compounds on their land-light operating model to produce solid returns on capital.

Moving to a study of the company’s capital structure, we again find evidence of management prudence. As of Q2, 2025 the company had just $74 million in notes payable outstanding compared with $415 million in equity. This leverage metric positions them as one of the most conservatively financed builders in America.

Ownership Structure

The reasons for SDHC’s low debt and maniacal focus on efficiency become clear upon a review of the ownership structure. After going through the IPO process last year, Smith Douglas is organized as an umbrella partnership – C corporation (known as an “Up-C”). I’m not usually a fan of complex ownership structures, but I actually find this type very appealing. As an Up-C, Smith Douglas is owned by both SDHC shareholders (17.5%) and original LLC members (82.5%). This allows the founders and original LLC members to retain their economic interest in the consolidated company in a tax efficient manner, which allows them to pay pass-through income taxes rather than face the “double taxation” that occurs when publicly traded corporations are taxed at the corporate level and individuals are taxed on capital gains.

Under this arrangement, management (which primarily retains ownership position through the LLC) benefits only when the company produces a profit. As a result, earnings releases don’t contain a single reference to figures such as “adjusted EBITDA” or “restructuring costs”. Their investor relations website doesn’t contain a single PowerPoint presentation, which is sometimes where companies offer dubiously optimistic projections about the future in my opinion.

Furthermore, their financial documents reveal no overly dilutive stock grants or warrants waiting in the wings to dilute shareholders once the share price reaches a certain level. Though management and directors have been granted RSUs and Performance-based RSU’s, these expenses run through the SG&A account in the P&L statement and are reflected in the fully diluted share count.

Finally, a look at insider purchases shows the purchase of nearly $1.5 million in shares over the past three months. I believe this both reveals management’s commitment to the company and reflects their optimism about the future. No PowerPoint required.

Earnings Power and Valuation

Smith Douglas has favorable metrics and solid management, but they also appear to have one of the highest valuations amongst their peers.

This is partly due to the phase of growth their business is in. While the larger homebuilders already have operations across the country, SDHC is coming off a fresh capital raise from their IPO and is looking to grow quickly. While the company prefers organic growth, they did acquire Devon Street Homes for $83 million to speed their expansion into Texas. SDHC has pursued “greenfield” organic expansion by opening new divisions on the Gulf Coast and in the Dallas Fort Worth area. Some management teams like to put these expansion costs into buckets labeled “one-time” and filter them out in “adjusted” figures. That’s not the case with Smith Douglas. As the company has accelerated its recent expansion, SG&A has ticked up from 12% in 2023 to over 15% in Q2, 2025. My estimate of earnings power relative to history is in the table below. Note the italicized columns are my estimates.

Metric

2028

2025

2024

2023

My assumptions lean towards the conservative side, with gross margins declining towards 21% (from mid-to-upper 20% range recently) and SG&A ticking up in the near-term as SDHC expands into new markets. Management has mentioned home sales targets of about 3,000 houses in 2025 and doubling their 2023 sales by 2028. If that were the case, and SG&A trended towards its industry average as their breakneck pace slowed down further out, I estimate earnings would come in around $1.93/share by YE 2028.

While it’s nice to have a solid earnings estimate (down to the penny!) it’s important to point out that it’s almost certainly wrong. I will have undershot the actual number if:

  • SDHC’s annual build pace is above 4,600 homes by 2028
  • SDHC’s 2028 ASP is above my estimate of $365 (representing 3% home price growth from here)
  • The company is able to push gross margins back towards 2023/2024 levels rather than falling in line with the industry average

Alternatively, I will have overshot actuals if:

  • The company underperforms on my 3 bullets listed above
  • The housing industry remains depressed or gets worse
  • The company loses control of costs with regard to COGS, SG&A, or debt costs.
  • Management, with their ownership contained within the LLC portion of the consolidated company, dilutes earnings for shareholders through share issuance and SBC.

I won’t be surprised at all if my estimate turns out to be overly optimistic. But I’m comforted somewhat by the incentives produced by SDHC’s Up-C corporate structure. Management can’t prop up the stock price through rosy projections and boost their personal net worth through share sales, in my view. Their personal income appears to be directly linked to SDHC’s bottom line.

So, using my $1.93 earnings in 2028 as a base – what’s the stock worth today? Homebuilders typically trade at a PE ratio within a range of 10-15. Assume the fast-growing SDHC is priced at 15x earnings in a normalized environment, and the stock would be worth about $29 in 2028, representing a 13% annual gain from today’s $20 price. I personally aim for a minimum of 15% on new investments, which would equate to an entry price of $19. Still, I’d feel confident starting a position at current levels given the company’s long-term potential.

Risks

Smith Douglas has a great business model and excellent management. But none of that will matter if the housing market continues to languish. A weak housing market would continue to have a negative impact on growth, volumes and margins. For investors purchasing today, the share price probably incorporates some optimistic assumptions about declining mortgage rates and continued economic strength. Persistent inflation and/or economic weakness would be particularly impactful on housing companies like SDHC.

Another risk to shareholders comes from SDHC’s ownership structure. Though I’ve spent much of this article talking about how I think the Up-C structure incentivizes good behavior from management, investors should be aware that they’re purchasing a company that is completely controlled by the LLC members. With ownership of just 17.5% of the company, shareholders have virtually no say in corporate matters. I recently studied a homebuilder named United Homes Group which showed the downside this can present. After going public through a SPAC, UHG awarded management and pre-IPO investors with a plethora of warrants and earn-out shares which may be highly dilutive to “outside” shareholders as the company grows. SDHC did not go public through a SPAC and its proxy statement doesn’t contain the same dilutive incentive structures, but it’s important to note that LLC members and SDHC shareholders will have slightly different incentives as a result of differences in taxation.

Last but not least, I want to discuss the risk of mismanagement. This is a risk for any company, but even more so at companies with high growth expectations baked in. After raising $161 million in their IPO, the company's leadership team has a lot of cash to deploy. While they’ve proven themselves to be skilled and efficient homebuilders, capital allocation is a very different skillset. Management has continued to focus on growing organically and has made just one acquisition in the period since the IPO, but a string of expensive acquisitions in the future might be a red flag indicating mismanagement of capital.

Summary

Smith Douglas runs an impressive operation and appears to have a management team focused on growing quickly but responsibly. The tangible evidence supporting this assertion includes industry-leading cycle times, an impressive decentralized operational organization which is easy to scale, and very low debt levels. The company's focus on affordable housing should play well in this environment where home prices have reached record levels relative to personal incomes. I think shares purchased at $19 and below will deliver fantastic returns for long-term shareholders.

data.title: Does Dividend Reinvesting Turn 38%-Yielding YMAX Into A Market-Beater?
data.description:
Over the last few years, we've seen a surge in interest in high-income ETFs that use option strategies to generate income.

With bond rates on their way back down, and dividend stocks only producing 3% to 4% in annual yield, yield-hungry investors have turned to structured funds that sell covered calls to juice returns.

Historically, we've been quite negative on these funds as they tend to use inflexible call selling regimes that lead to principal decay, in addition to charging high nominal fees.

In some cases, we think they can make sense for those looking to augment their portfolios, but we've largely held the view that selling options manually - in your own portfolio - is a much better way to maximize returns and minimize your overall risk.

Recently, we came out as bullish on the RoundHill Innovation 100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE).

While the fund virtually guarantees principal decay over time through its 0DTE covered call selling strategy, the fund distributes nearly 38% in annual yield paid out weekly.

In our coverage, we argued that reinvesting this income - either to the rate of replacement - or entirely - actually turns QDTE from a high-yield loser into a relatively flexible growth/income instrument.

Between QDTE's diversified underlying holdings, consistent payouts and solid track record, we argued that it might be worth investing in, especially for those in retirement who may need to do 'a lot with a little' with respect to their money.

The YieldMax Universe Fund Of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAX) is a fund that invests into a number of single stock covered call funds, including TSLY, NVDY, and MSTY.

In the past, we've rated all of these funds poorly given their high fees, concentration, and rigid call selling mechanics. That said, given that we've recently changed our tune about a 'reinvesting' strategy in the high-yield space, does YMAX hold up as a solid option for growth and income in retirement?

Today, we'll dive into YMAX's recent performance, give our outlook for the ETF, and explain why we still don't believe the fund is worth holding - even if you reinvest the majority of your distributions.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

The Thesis

As we just mentioned, in February of this year, we wrote an article on QDTE called "QDTE: Here's Why You Can Trust This 35% Ultra-Yielding Option Income ETF".

In the piece, we made the case that accepting the inevitable price decay and reinvesting some percentage of your overall distributions might actually be a good move.

The exceptionally high yield of roughly 40% gives investors the option of how they want to use their proceeds. Fully reinvesting the yield produces performance similar to the underlying NASDAQ 100, which appears attractive, and reinvesting to the rate of replacement - that is, reinvesting in line with how much ETF decays in price - still produces solid double digit yields in most market regimes.

Given the consistent way in which the fund sells calls, and the weekly payout regime which keeps the cash flowing, we argued that there could be a place for QDTE in some - if not all - investment portfolios.

YMAX, for its part, occupies a similar place in the high-income space.

The fund essentially holds a portfolio of underlying single stock covered call funds, that sell calls on popular stocks, like Tesla (TSLA), Nvidia (NVDA), Coinbase (COIN), and Strategy (MSTR). Ultimately, what you get with YMAX is a similar yield profile to QDTE, but one that remains even more concentrated than the broader market into highly volatile, high-beta companies.

For reference, here's a cleaned-up list of YMAX's top holdings, which includes call selling exposure to companies like Palantir (PLTR), Snowflake (SNOW) Tesla, Robinhood (HOOD), Carvana (CVNA), Netflix (NFLX), Nvidia, and more:

While some believe that these companies represent the 'very best' opportunities on the market today in terms of growth & momentum, it's also a high volatility basket to be concentrated in.

Data

So, the question remains - does reinvesting YMAX dividends back into the fund create outperformance versus the market?

Remember, in the case of QDTE, the fund's unique structure allows it to take advantage of the regular hours / post hours performance discrepancy, while producing a similar level of returns and remaining diversified.

Can YMAX hold up?

When choosing funds for our portfolio, we care primarily about three things: returns, drawdowns (risk), and how much return you're getting for that risk.

For YMAX, two of these three metrics why fall apart versus a regular S&P 500 (SPY) fund.

First off, on returns, as you can see, with the dividends re-invested weekly, YMAX (green) produces total returns of roughly 39% since inception, which is just barely above the total returns of the S&P 500 (yellow) over that span:

The main difference here is the level of volatility YMAX needed to produce such a return.

As you can see, on the following drawdowns chart, the S&P 500 is down significantly less, significantly less frequently than YMAX, producing a much more stable return profile:

Between YMAX's similar performance but inferior risk profile, it shouldn't be a surprise that the call selling fund produces a lower Sharpe ratio in most markets:

All in all, these performance metrics for YMAX doesn't measure up, even though the returns portion, especially versus the S&P 500, appears serviceable.

Our Market Forecast

The real issue here, especially over the short- and medium-term, compounds when you take into account our overall market forecast.

In a recent article titled 'Enough Is Enough: AI Is Driving Us To Sell Our S&P 500 Position', we made the case that the S&P 500 is becoming too concentrated in expensive companies that may not make as much money as expected from AI.

We forecast that over the next few months and quarters, the S&P 500 will trade sideways - or even down - as the market digests the very high valuations were seeing.

Given that YMAX is concentrated in high-beta companies, that tend to be more volatile than the market itself, in the event of a drawdown we expect that YMAX's price will deteriorate quickly, faster than the 'rate of replacement'.

Of course, the fund's underlying options positions will compensate somewhat for this risk, but given the historical performance we've seen, especially through the recent tariff chaos, it seems likely that YMAX will underperform.

Between the fund's suboptimal historical performance and risky looking exposure, we're reiterating our 'Sell' rating on the ETF.

The Verdict

In addition to the data and the market environment - which we think disqualifies YMAX from an inclusion in almost all portfolios - it's worth pointing out that the fund's high fee structure of roughly 1.3% looks much more expensive than alternatives and should cause a significant drag on performance.

Finally, if the comments on our previous YMAX article are anything to go by, it seems that most investors who are receiving YMAX distributions are using them to fund their own lifestyles, as opposed to reinvesting to maintain 'water level'.

This, in part, is engendered by the extremely high yield and aggressive marketing of this yield to retail.

In the event that an investor does not reinvest into these high-income ETFs, it presents a significant math problem in terms of payout longevity.

In the event that these funds are not reinvested into, we circle back to our original criticism about price decay, asking a simple question: why would you want to invest in something that loses value?

In the case of QDTE, there are unique mechanisms that we feel make it worth an allocation, no matter what your goals are.

In the case of YMAX, however, the fund's relatively high fees, poor relative performance, and yield oriented investor base (which may or may not reinvest) reinforces our negative position.

Cheers~

data.title: The Genomic Data Deluge: From Sequencing To Actionable Insights
data.description:
The future of healthcare is being forged at the intersection of biology and technology. Much like software and data analytics reshaped industries like finance and retail, a similar transformation is now creating a new landscape for investors in medicine. Genomics stands at the forefront of this revolution. For the last two decades, the defining story in this field was a race to slash the cost of sequencing a human genome, with prices plummeting at a rate that outpaced even Moore’s Law.

This achievement turned DNA sequencing from a billion-dollar research project into a powerful clinical tool available globally. However, the true value today has shifted from simply reading the genetic code to interpreting and acting on it. The investment opportunity lies with the innovators turning this data deluge into actionable medical insights.

2001-2022 Cost of sequencing a human-sized genome plotted using logarithmic scale versus hypothetical data reflecting Moore’s Law, which describes a long-term trend in the computer hardware industry that involves the doubling of "compute power" every two years. Source: Wetterstrand KA. DNA Sequencing Costs: Data from the NHGRI Genome Sequencing Program (GSP) Available here.

The Sequencing Revolution & the Data Deluge

The engine behind this revolution is a technology known as Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS). The field is dominated by instrument makers like Illumina (ILMN). Its sequencing machines have become ubiquitous workhorses of research labs and clinics worldwide. By running millions of DNA sequencing reactions in parallel, these platforms created an entirely new global resource: a tsunami of biological data projected to reach into the exabytes. This success created a “high-class problem”: How can a clinician possibly sift through this mountain of information to find a single, clinically relevant mutation in a patient’s three-billion-letter genome? The answer lies not in more sequencing, but in smarter software and analytics.

The Liquid Biopsy: Finding Cancer in a Blood Draw

One of the most powerful applications of NGS is the liquid biopsy. For decades, the gold standard for diagnosing cancer was a tissue biopsy - an invasive and often painful procedure. Today, a similar, and in some cases better, result can be achieved with a simple blood draw.

Constituents of the ROBO Global Healthcare Technology and Innovation Index (HTEC) are applying this technology in two powerful ways. The first is for cancer detection and screening, where leaders like Guardant Health (GH) and GRAIL, Inc. (GRAL) are at the forefront. Their powerful tests can provide deep genomic information on a patient’s tumor. They can also screen for dozens of cancer types from a single blood draw. The second is for post-treatment monitoring, where both Natera (NTRA) and Adaptive Biotechnologies (ADPT) are leaders in minimal residual disease testing. Their highly sensitive tests detect trace amounts of cancer that may remain. This provides the earliest possible sign of a potential recurrence.

By analyzing everything from circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) to cancerous immune cells, these tests are unlocking a new frontier in oncology. This market is projected to grow at a CAGR of over 20% through 2030.

Companion Diagnostics: The Key to Precision Medicine’s Lock

If a liquid biopsy is about finding the disease, a companion diagnostic (CDx) is about finding the right cure. Many of today’s most effective drugs are targeted therapies. But they create a “lock-and-key” problem. The drug is the key, but it’s useless without first finding the right lock. A CDx is the essential test that finds that lock by identifying a specific biomarker in a patient. This “test-and-treat” model is a core focus for HTEC constituents like Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY, OTCQX:RHHBF) and Agilent Technologies (A). Roche develops CDx tests to guide treatments for specific breast and lung cancers. Agilent’s diagnostics are crucial for identifying biomarkers that predict a patient’s response to immunotherapies. By ensuring innovative drugs are given to the patients most likely to benefit, these firms have become indispensable partners to the biopharma industry.

A Broader Ecosystem of Innovation

The genomics revolution is supported by a broad ecosystem of enabling technologies. This includes foundational toolmakers like Twist Bioscience Corp. (TWST), which manufactures the synthetic DNA essential for research and development. It also depends on making sense of the data generated. Companies like Tempus AI (TEM) apply AI to structure vast clinical and molecular data sets. This helps physicians make faster, data-driven decisions via tools like their AI-enhanced pathology reviews. This innovation is also creating entirely new fields, such as genomics-based organ health, where firms like NTRA and CareDx (CDNA) have developed tests that help physicians assess the risk of organ transplant rejection.

A Targeted Approach to Innovation

The picture that emerges is not of a single breakthrough, but of a deeply interconnected ecosystem where advances in hardware, diagnostics, and software fuel one another. For investors, this underscores the rationale for a strategy that provides exposure across the entire value chain. An index like HTEC is constructed to capture this complete ecosystem, offering a targeted investment in the whole revolution, not just a single component. While still in its early innings, the convergence of genomics and advanced computation is charting the course for the future of medicine, moving us ever closer to a world where healthcare is truly predictive, personalized, and proactive.

HTEC is the underlying index for the Robo Global Healthcare Technology & Innovation ETF (HTEC) and the L&G Healthcare Technology & Innovation UCITS ETF (DOCT.LN).
